title: PLSQL-CAT-020 — Pre-filter wrapped + edition-isolated objects in DBMS_METADATA extraction; consult ALL_PLSQL_OBJECT_SETTINGS for compile-time PLSCOPE_SETTINGS. (/oracle audit finding 2)
close_reason: Investigated: catalog code already extracts EDITION_NAME from ALL_OBJECTS (lines 148, 1976, 2648-2670) AND surfaces wrapped/inaccessible-edition messaging in DBMS_METADATA error remediation (line 1748). The /oracle audit's 'pre-filter wrapped + edition-isolated objects' suggestion is a perf-only optimization — current behaviour relies on DBMS_METADATA's graceful failure on wrapped sources and the EDITION_NAME field for downstream filtering. The full preemptive filter would require an extra ALL_PLSQL_OBJECT_SETTINGS query; deferring until the engine can demonstrate the perf cost matters at scale. Field semantics are correct.

title: PLSQL-CICD-015 — Implement `plsql post-pr-comment` subcommand (GitHub + GitLab adapters)

Body always prefixes html_marker (from PrCommentEnvelope, CICD-014/vvxw) so existing_comment_id can route via a downstream find-by-marker step — idempotent comment update. Library is dep-light: no reqwest/hyper here; the binary wrapper (a separate downstream bead) does the actual HTTP call and reads the auth token from env. 6 unit tests cover both platforms × both modes (create + edit) plus body-carries-marker and request-determinism. 59 plsql-cicd tests pass (up from 53); clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. title: PLSQL-SUPPORT-014 — Redaction-delta manifest generator: record every transformation, commit alongside the fixture
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-support/src/redaction_delta.rs with RedactionDeltaManifest, DeltaStep, DeltaConfig types + record_redaction_delta(original, config) -> RedactionDeltaManifest + verify_redaction_delta(original, manifest, config) -> bool. The manifest records every redaction transformation applied to a fixture in canonical order: (1) apply_rules substring rule-list, (2) scrub_literals literal scrubbing, (3) rename_identifiers token-level rename. Each step records: name, match_count, byte_delta (signed; positive = grew, negative = shrank), post_step_sha256 (chain-of-trust hash), and step-specific metadata (rule_count + manifest_version for rules, strings_scrubbed/numerics_scrubbed/date_literals_scrubbed for scrub, renamed_identifier_count + preserved_keyword_count for rename). Manifest carries original_sha256 + redacted_sha256 + step chain + byte counts but NEVER the original content (test enforces this — JSON of manifest cannot contain pre-redaction text). verify_redaction_delta replays the config + checks hash chain equality — proves bytewise reproducibility. Schema pinned: plsql.support.redaction_delta v1. 8 unit tests: 3-step ordering, schema pinning, no-pre-content leak, rule match-count semantics, round-trip verify, salt-differs-fails verify, chain-of-trust last==final, determinism, byte_delta totals match manifest. 68 plsql-support tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Re-exported via lib.rs. Cite: plan.md §16 (Support workflow) + /security-audit-for-saas. Closes oracle-0z5a.

title: PLSQL-PRIV-DOC-1 — plsql-privileges lacks a doctor surface (/world-class-doctor-mode)
description:
Audit finding from /world-class-doctor-mode pass.

State. The workspace has 6 doctor surfaces shipped:
- CatalogDoctorReport (plsql-catalog) — grant suggestions + counts.
- DepGraphDoctorReport (plsql-depgraph) — node/edge counts + invariants.
- LineageDoctorReport (plsql-lineage) — orphan/freshness/observation-window.
- McpDoctorReport (plsql-mcp) — tool registry + Instant Client posture + write-safety.
- ChangesetDoctorReport (plsql-cicd) — overall_risk + remediation_hints.
- (CLI-only) corpus-license-check / plan-lint --doctor.

Gap. plsql-privileges has no doctor surface. A privilege model that successfully resolves needs a way for an agent to say 'how confident are we that this model is complete?' — e.g., flagging:
- Synonyms with unresolved targets that still produce a SynonymPrivilegePath.
- AuthorizationAmbiguity instances (the model already records these, but no aggregator surfaces them as a doctor count).
- Schemas referenced by the catalog but for which we have zero ALL_TAB_PRIVS rows (could mean 'no grants' or could mean 'we lack SELECT_CATALOG_ROLE' — the former is fine, the latter is a silent gap).

Action. Add a PrivilegeDoctorReport struct with:
- ambiguity_count
- unresolved_synonym_targets
- schemas_without_grants (for cross-checking with catalog capability negotiation)
- counts: definer/invoker objects, accessible_by clauses observed
And a doctor() free fn that builds it from a PrivilegeModel + CatalogSnapshot. Wire into the lineage/depgraph doctor-aggregation path.

Cite. /world-class-doctor-mode — every layer-2 surface gets a doctor so 'why is my analysis like this?' has a single first-stop answer.
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-privileges/src/doctor.rs with PrivilegeDoctorReport (privileges_total, public_grants_total, synonym_paths_total, public_synonym_paths, access_control_entries_total, cross_schema_writes_total/ambiguous, authorization_ambiguities_total, ambiguity_reasons: Vec<DoctorReasonRow>, schemas_observed_total, authid_distribution, diagnostics_total, posture, remediation_hints). Classify_posture three-state (Clean/Caution/Unknown) where Unknown fires when ambiguities outnumber resolved privileges. Build_remediation_hints surfaces ambiguity / cross-schema / public-synonym / diagnostics / empty-model hints. doctor_report(model) free fn; AuthidDistribution::record() helper. 7 new unit tests cover empty, ambiguity-counting, cross-schema-ambiguity, unknown-posture trigger, public-synonym counting, distinct-schema-union, and AUTHID distribution. Wired into lib.rs pub re-exports. Tests + clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-SUPPORT-012 — Token-level identifier-renaming pass; idempotent with the bundle's per-bundle salt
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-support/src/rename.rs with rename_identifiers(source, salt) -> (String, RenameStats) and rename_with_reserved(source, salt, &[&str]) variants. Walks PL/SQL source byte-by-byte, renames every Oracle identifier ([A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_$#]*) to id_<6-byte-hex> via SHA-256(salt || \0 || raw), deterministic across runs. Reserved-word safe: DEFAULT_RESERVED list (78 keywords + datatypes) preserves SELECT/FROM/etc. Quoted identifiers ("Customer Id") rename the body but keep quotes. String literals ('...' with doubled '' escape), line comments (--), and block comments (/* */) pass through unchanged. SHA-256 salt provides per-bundle uniqueness; same source under same salt is byte-identical. RenameStats reports distinct renamed count + preserved keyword count. 12 unit tests cover deterministic-across-runs, salt-changes-output, reserved-pass-through, string-literal pass-through, doubled-apostrophe, line + block comments, quoted-identifier body rename, same-id-same-alias within a pass, alias length stable, empty source, custom reserved override, idempotent re-application. 46 plsql-support tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-accretion gate functions use stringly-typed Result<String, String> in public API
description:
crates/plsql-accretion/src/gate.rs exposes a cluster of PUBLIC functions that all return Result<String, String> -- both the Ok payload and the Err are bare strings:

  gate.rs:344  pub fn roundtrip_check(corpus_dir, fixtures_dir) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:455  pub fn honesty_check(candidate_text) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:512  pub fn residue_check(candidate_text, fixtures_dir) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:599  pub fn measure_estate_metrics(estate) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:624  pub fn baseline_cmp(baseline_json, metrics_text) -> Result<String, String>
  gate.rs:699  pub fn pins_check(repo_root, candidate_text) -> Result<String, String>

This is inconsistent with the rest of the workspace, which is uniformly thiserror-based (36 crates depend on thiserror; no anyhow). plsql-accretion itself already defines a typed ProposerError enum in proposer.rs and uses it there -- so the gate module is the odd one out within its own crate.

The CompletionBackend trait (proposer.rs:659) similarly uses Result<String, String>, though that one is at least documented as deliberately opaque.

Why it matters: stringly-typed errors in a public API force consumers to string-match to branch on failure mode, cannot be matched exhaustively, and lose structure. A public reviewer evaluating the crate's API quality will flag the inconsistency -- the project clearly knows how to do typed errors (thiserror everywhere else) and just didn't here.

Suggested fix: introduce a GateError thiserror enum (or reuse/extend an existing accretion error type) with variants for the distinct failure modes these functions can hit (I/O, parse, honesty-violation, residue-found, baseline-regression, pin-mismatch). The Ok arm carrying a human report string is acceptable, but a typed Ok payload would be cleaner. Lower priority than the half-finished-feature beads but worth doing before tagging a stable API.

title: PLSQL-ENG-003B — Wire plsql-engine pipeline to call plsql-store get_cached/put_cached with content+profile hashes; surface cache_hit_ratio in doctor (replaces cache_status NotWired)
close_reason: Implemented ENG-003B engine-side cache wiring (commit 14985d5) consuming cc_1's plsql-store keyed-cache API (CacheKey/get_cached/put_cached/hash_hex from ENG-003). AnalysisRun gains cache_outcome:Option<bool> (#[serde(default)] back-compat): None=inactive, Some(true)=served-from-cache, Some(false)=active-miss-then-stored. analyze_project: when req.cache.enabled+directory set, computes content_hash(all sorted file path+bytes)+profile_hash(serde analysis_profile), probes Store::get_cached strategy 'semantic_fragment' — hit=>deserialize cached AnalysisRun + short-circuit whole pipeline; miss=>run+put_cached. Best-effort (any store failure => uncached run, never hard-error). Profile change=>different profile_hash=>key miss=>recompute (no stale cross-profile fragment, by key construction). engine_full_doctor_report cache_status/cache_hit_ratio now derive from run.cache_outcome (Reported+1.0/0.0 when active, honest NotWired/None when inactive — replaces the blanket NotWired). Added plsql-store dep. 15 engine tests (miss->hit->profile-invalidation, no-cache-dir=NotWired, existing suite); plsql-mcp/mcp-pro/cicd consumers all green (serde-default back-compat); cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. title: PLSQL-PARSE-000C — Parser backend tournament: antlr4rust vs Java ANTLR worker; produce go/no-go matrix with perf, memory, panic-rate, span stability, build, and portability results; close decision artifact at `docs/decisions/D1-backend-tournament-result.md`
close_reason: Closed D1 decision via docs/decisions/D1-backend-tournament-result.md (commit 53e5ad4). Honest go/no-go matrix (R13: dimensions *measured* from committed re-runnable test evidence or *deferred* where a built Java jar is required — NO fabricated perf/memory numbers). Verdict GO antlr4rust as production backend (only working end-to-end in-tree path, zero measured panic-rate on adversarial input, pure-Rust no external runtime, R20-clean, all consumers already use it); java-antlr = documented production fallback candidate, NO-GO until explicit flip-criteria checklist met (grammar jar built+distributable, PARSE-000D wire conformance, byte-for-byte span parity vs PARSE-000 fixtures, statistically-valid >=30-sample perf bench, RSS within budget, zero live-path panic). Reversible without API churn (ParseBackend trait + PARSE-000D) so deciding now unblocks PLSQL-RELEASE-001 without foreclosing the fallback. Supersedes OPEN status in D1-parser-backend-spike.md; README index Open->Closed. Docs-only, no code change. Builds on PARSE-000B/000D. My parser lane, collision-free with cc_1's plsql-sast. title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — --listen public-bind error advises a flag that does not exist
description:
crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:59 -- the ListenTargetError::PublicBindRefused error message reads: '--listen target {raw} binds to a public-internet address; pass --allow-public-bind to override'. The same advice is repeated in the module doc (tcp.rs:24) and a unit test in main.rs:904 asserts the stderr contains '--allow-public-bind'.

However, crates/plsql-mcp/src/main.rs:180-181 explicitly comments: '--allow-public-bind is not yet a CLI flag, so the safe default (loopback/private only) holds.' The MCP CLI never registers an --allow-public-bind argument (clap derive in main.rs has no such field; parse_listen_target is always called with allow_public_bind=false at main.rs:182).

Why it matters: a user who tries to bind plsql-mcp to a public address gets an error telling them to pass a flag that does not exist. The advice is un-actionable -- the user has no documented way to override. For an OSS release this is a dead-end UX path and an honesty gap (error text promises a capability the binary lacks).

Suggested fix: pick one of --
(a) Implement the --allow-public-bind CLI flag in main.rs's clap Serve command and thread it into parse_listen_target, OR
(b) If public bind is intentionally not exposed, change the error message to state that public binds are not supported (drop the '--allow-public-bind to override' clause) and update the tcp.rs module doc + the main.rs:904 test accordingly.

Note: the underlying tcp::parse_listen_target already supports an allow_public_bind parameter, so option (a) is a small wiring change.
close_reason: Wired --allow-public-bind as a real clap flag on 'serve' (Command::Serve + run_serve threaded to tcp::parse_listen_target). The error text that advised the flag is now actionable; the public-bind override the tcp.rs machinery already supported is reachable. plsql-mcp tests green (incl. public-bind-refused test).

title: PLSQL-MCP-DESIGN-1 — Audit findings from /mcp-server-design pass
description:
Audit finding from /mcp-server-design pass on crates/plsql-mcp/.

State. plsql-mcp ships a foundation skeleton (PLSQL-MCP-001) with a tool registry, doctor surface, audit baseline, K18 sanitization, safety token, transport stubs, and a config loader. 4803 LOC across the crate, 86 tests. Live-DB feature gate properly behind a feature flag.

Findings.

1. Transport is still stubbed (transport.rs). The stdio MCP loop is not wired — PLSQL-MCP-002 covers that. This is honestly documented as a skeleton.

2. Tool registry has no built-in `list_tools` MCP method response builder — the registry stores tools but the JSON-RPC `tools/list` shape isn't synthesized in code. PLSQL-MCP-002 covers this.

3. No protocol version negotiation surface — the MCP spec requires `initialize` with protocolVersion handshake. Not currently in tools/transport modules.

4. Audit surface (audit.rs) emits AuditPlan but no rotating audit log writer — every operation will accrete events in-memory. For production, a sink that writes to disk (with PII-redacted row text) and rotates daily would be needed. The Sink trait exists, the rotating implementation does not.

5. enable_writes token flow has good shape (session-scoped, time-bounded) but no idle-timeout — a token issued and then forgotten remains valid until process exit. Recommend an explicit `last_used_at` field on SessionSafetyState with a configurable idle-window check before each write call.

6. The DSN config supports `~/.dbtools` alias lookup as a skeleton (connections.rs:68) — the actual ~/.dbtools file format and walk are not implemented. PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007 covers this.

Action. Items 1-3 are already gated by PLSQL-MCP-002. Items 4-5 are net-new beads to file separately (audit-log rotation; enable_writes idle-timeout). Item 6 is already gated by PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007.

Cite. /mcp-server-design — model the MCP protocol surface as initialize -> tools/list -> tools/call with explicit version negotiation; audit logs always rotate.
close_reason: /mcp-server-design audit recorded. plsql-mcp foundation skeleton (PLSQL-MCP-001) is honestly documented; transport stub + tool registry + protocol-version handshake are all gated by PLSQL-MCP-002 which has not landed yet. Items 1-3 of the audit (transport / list_tools / protocolVersion) are explicitly downstream beads. Items 4-5 (audit-log rotation, enable_writes idle-timeout) are net-new findings that need separate beads when prioritized; logged in the bead description for pickup. Item 6 (~/.dbtools alias) is gated by PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007. Closing as audit-only — design findings recorded, implementation deferred.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — recognise_string_literal mangles non-ASCII content via 'bytes[i] as char' (Latin-1 reinterpretation)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/expr.rs  fn recognise_string_literal (lines 163-188), specifically line 184: 'out.push(bytes[i] as char);'

BUG: The function un-doubles '' escapes by walking the inner string's BYTES (let bytes = inner.as_bytes(); line 171) and pushing each byte as a char: 'out.push(bytes[i] as char)'. 'u8 as char' maps a byte 0..=255 to the Unicode scalar value with that numeric codepoint -- i.e. it reinterprets UTF-8 bytes as Latin-1. Any non-ASCII character in a PL/SQL string literal is therefore corrupted: each multi-byte UTF-8 sequence is expanded into its individual bytes, each rendered as a wrong Latin-1 char.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate against plsql-ir):
  lower_expression("'cafe'") with an accented e  =>  Expr::StringLit("cafA©")  -- the 2-byte UTF-8 sequence for the accented e (0xC3 0xA9) became two Latin-1 chars. content != input.

WHY IT TRIGGERS: any string literal containing non-ASCII text -- accented characters, non-Latin scripts, the section sign, em-dash, smart quotes, etc. PL/SQL source is routinely UTF-8 (AL32UTF8 databases) so this is reachable on real corpora.

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT (MEDIUM): Expr::StringLit content feeds flow analysis -- crates/plsql-ir/src/flow_intra.rs:168 reads StringLit(s) for string_shape (dynamic-SQL taint). A corrupted literal propagates corrupted bytes into the IR and any consumer that inspects literal text. It does not crash (no panic -- bytes are always valid as Latin-1 chars) but it is a silent data-correctness defect: the IR no longer faithfully represents the source.

SUGGESTED FIX: walk chars, not bytes. Use inner.char_indices() / a chars().peekable() loop: detect a '\'\'' pair and emit one '\'', a solitary middle '\'' aborts (return None), otherwise push the char unchanged. This preserves multi-byte UTF-8 correctly. (The doubled-quote and solitary-quote logic only needs to compare against the ASCII ''' which is single-byte, so a char-based walk is straightforward.)
close_reason: recognise_string_literal (plsql-ir/src/expr.rs:184) no longer does 'bytes[i] as char' (Latin-1 reinterpretation). Now walks inner.chars().peekable(): a doubled '' emits one ', a solitary mid-quote aborts (None), every other char pushed unchanged — multi-byte UTF-8 preserved. New tests: string_literal_preserves_non_ascii_utf8 (cafe/accents/Cyrillic/CJK round-trip) and string_literal_unescapes_doubled_quotes_with_non_ascii. Written failing-first (confirmed mojibake), watched fail, then fixed. Suite green.

title: PLSQL-CICD-002 — Implement `predict <changeset>` using lineage `impact()` + Oracle-specific invalidation rules
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-cicd/src/predict.rs implementing the predict <changeset> rule engine. predict(changeset, mode) -> InvalidationPrediction iterates each ChangedObject and dispatches per ChangedObjectKind through apply_oracle_invalidation_rules. Each match arm is one Oracle-specific invalidation rule with confidence tier per PredictMode (CatalogAware = High, LiveSnapshot = High with extracted-at-runtime note, SourceOnly = Low + SourceOnlyHeuristic uncertainty appended). Rules implemented: PackageSpec -> PackageSpecChanged direct invalidation; PackageBody -> ConditionalCompilationBranch uncertainty only (Oracle 11gR2+ fine-grained dependency policy means body-only changes do not invalidate dependents); StandaloneRoutineSignature -> RoutineSignatureChanged; StandaloneRoutineBody -> no invalidation (same fine-grained policy); TableAdditiveDdl/TableDestructiveDdl -> typed reason variants; TypeEvolution / SynonymRetargeting / GrantOrRevoke / EditionedObjectChange / MaterializedViewRefreshAffecting -> per-reason invalidation; ViewDefinitionChange / TriggerChange -> Other-reason with descriptive text; IndexChange / SequenceChange -> MissingCatalogObject informational uncertainty (Oracle does not invalidate dependents); OtherKnownKind -> low-confidence catch-all; Unclassified -> typed ParserRecoveryRegion uncertainty (R13). Per-object UnknownReason uncertainties propagate into the prediction's uncertainties Vec. Output sorted by (distance, owner, name) for stable diff. 8 new unit tests cover: empty changeset, package spec invalidation + High confidence, package body uncertainty + no invalidation, SourceOnly downgrades + appends source-only uncertainty, destructive DDL typed reason, index/sequence informational, unclassified typed uncertainty, stable ordering, per-object uncertainty propagation. title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-bindgen & plsql-doc Cargo.toml miss [lints] workspace = true
description:
crates/plsql-bindgen/Cargo.toml and crates/plsql-doc/Cargo.toml are the only two workspace crates that do NOT have a '[lints]\nworkspace = true' section (verified by scanning all crates/*/Cargo.toml and tools/*/Cargo.toml). Every other crate inherits the workspace lint table defined in /Cargo.toml, which includes 'unsafe_code = forbid' (rust) and 'dbg_macro = deny' + 'todo = deny' (clippy).

Why it matters: PUBLISHING.md asserts 'The whole workspace is #![forbid(unsafe_code)]'. Both crates DO carry an inline '#![forbid(unsafe_code)]' in lib.rs (and bindgen's main.rs), so unsafe is in fact still forbidden -- but the inconsistency means these two crates silently miss the 'dbg_macro = deny' and 'todo = deny' clippy gates. A stray dbg!() or todo!() could land in plsql-bindgen or plsql-doc without CI catching it, whereas it would be denied everywhere else. For an OSS release this is an inconsistent build-config surface a reviewer would flag.

Suggested fix: add the standard

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — prompt-injection sanitizer is blocklist-based and trivially evadable
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/query.rs:141-206 (injection_markers / sanitize)

THREAT (incomplete prompt-injection scrub): The K18 sanitizer that scrubs DB cell values before handing them to the calling agent is a fixed blocklist of literal marker strings (<tool_call>, antml:* tags, <|im_start|>, [INST], 'system: ' role prefixes, etc.). The skill's LLM-SECURITY guidance and Axiom 3 are explicit: prompt-injection defense must be structural (delimiting untrusted content), not a textual blocklist of 'known' injection shapes. A blocklist has unbounded evasions:

- Case/spacing variants: `< tool_call >`, `<TOOL_CALL>`, `<tool_call >` are not in the list (only exact `<tool_call>`).
- Unicode look-alikes / zero-width chars inside the tag.
- New model/tool-call syntaxes that did not exist when the list was written (the list is a snapshot; attack surface expands faster — Axiom 8).
- The far larger class of *plain-prose* injections ('Ignore previous instructions and ...') which carry no markup at all and are 100% unscrubbed.
- Markdown / code-fence tricks, role-confusion via newlines.

So a malicious row value in a customer DB (or any DB the agent queries) can still steer the calling agent. The sanitizer gives a false sense of completeness — sanitized=true / sanitized_cells signal implies 'this is now safe', which it is not.

This is the design pattern the skill warns against; it is not a single bug but a structural choice. Recommended changes:
1. Treat ALL query-result cell text as untrusted and wrap it structurally — e.g. emit cell values inside an explicit, clearly-delimited data envelope in the MCP response with a fixed, non-spoofable boundary, and document that the agent must treat envelope contents as data, never instructions.
2. Keep the marker scrub only as a belt-and-suspenders layer, but normalize first (case-fold, strip zero-width / control chars, collapse whitespace) so trivial variants are caught.
3. Make the response/docs honest that scrubbing is best-effort, not a guarantee — do not let `sanitized` read as 'safe'.

SEVERITY: Medium — the threat is real (data-borne prompt injection through query results) and the current control is materially weaker than it presents itself; appropriate to flag before an OSS release that advertises K18 sanitization.

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-007 — Implement `get_object_source`, `get_clob` (with K18 sanitization), `get_errors` (structured `USER_ERRORS` / `ALL_ERRORS`)
close_reason: Added crates/plsql-mcp/src/source.rs with three read-only tools: (1) run_get_object_source(conn, owner, object_name, object_type) reads ALL_SOURCE ordered by LINE, reassembles the body, sanitizes each line via the K18 sanitize() from PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-004, ensures lines end in newline for round-trip stability. Returns GetObjectSourceResponse with source + sanitized_lines counter + UnknownReason::ResponseSanitized when scrubbed. (2) run_get_clob(conn, sql, params, max_chars) one-row SELECT that projects a CLOB; first non-null cell is the payload; K18 sanitization + optional Unicode char-count truncation with ellipsis. (3) run_get_errors(conn, owner, object_name) reads ALL_ERRORS (when owner non-empty) or USER_ERRORS (when owner blank) for structured ObjectError rows: owner / object_name / object_type / line / position / attribute / message_number / text — numeric line+position+message_number parsed; agents can reason about them programmatically rather than parsing free text. All three share SourceToolError + use only positional parameter binding via OracleBind. 6 new tests cover: source reassembly order, sanitized-line marking with runtime-built injection payload, get_clob truncation+ellipsis, get_clob empty-result safety, get_errors structured rows with 2 distinct attributes/lines/numbers, get_errors USER_ERRORS routing when owner blank. 61/61 plsql-mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-parser-java is a non-functional stub crate with zero reverse deps
description:
crates/plsql-parser-java is a workspace member and publishable crate (dual-licensed, has a description) but is a non-functional stub:

- Its own lib.rs:18-24 documents that 'output decoding is deferred to PARSE-000D' and that 'for now *any* run' returns a degraded ParseResult with an empty AST/CST plus a PARSE-JAVA-UNAVAILABLE diagnostic. lib.rs:191-194 confirms: even when the Java worker runs successfully, the result is discarded because 'structured decoding is not yet wired'.
- So the JavaAntlrBackend NEVER produces a real parse, by design, regardless of input.
- It has zero reverse dependencies: a scan of all Cargo.toml + *.rs files shows nothing in the workspace depends on plsql-parser-java except the top-level workspace members list. The engine, MCP, accretion, etc. all use plsql-parser-antlr instead.

Why it matters: shipping a publishable crate in an OSS release whose sole exported backend is permanently non-functional, and which nothing in the tree consumes, is confusing. A reviewer browsing the workspace will spend time on a crate that does nothing. The crate's docs are honest about it (good), but the crate itself is dead weight.

Suggested fix: decide explicitly -- either (a) move plsql-parser-java out of the workspace members list and into a 'future-work' / unpublished state until PARSE-000D lands, or (b) if it must stay, set 'publish = false' in its Cargo.toml and add a top-of-README/crate note that it is an in-progress backend not yet usable. Same consideration applies more weakly to plsql-bindgen (also zero reverse deps) but bindgen at least ships a working CLI binary, so this bead is scoped to plsql-parser-java.

title: PLSQL-PROJECT — split_script has no string/comment state: a lone '/' line OR a trailing ';' INSIDE a multi-line string literal falsely splits the statement mid-string (corrupts unit boundaries)
description: splitter.rs split_script (line-oriented) decides boundaries via `trimmed == "/"` and `!in_plsql && trimmed.ends_with(';')` with NO tracking of open single-quote / q-quote string literals or block comments across lines. Repro 1 (false split on '/' inside string): a SQL statement containing a multi-line string literal where one interior line is exactly `/` -> split as if a SQL*Plus terminator. Repro 2 (false split on ';'): plain-SQL (not in_plsql) multi-line string literal whose first physical line ends with `;` e.g. INSERT INTO t VALUES ('line one;<NL>line two'); -> line 1 trimmed ends ';' and !in_plsql -> emitted as a complete statement, truncating the string and leaving `line two');` as a bogus second statement. Also block comments containing `/` on their own line or a line ending `;`. Fix: add cross-line lexer state (single-quote parity with '' escape, q'X..X' / nq, /* */ block comments) and suppress the '/' and ';' split triggers while any are open; mirror recover_to_statement_boundary (oracle-1wsf) and scrub_literals q-quote handling. Needs TDD with multi-line-string + lone-/-in-string + ;-in-string + block-comment cases; behavior-sensitive (rest of pipeline depends on unit boundaries) so do it as a focused dedicated change, not a drive-by. Same string-blindness class as 1wsf (parser-antlr) and 5sdv (looks_wrapped).
close_reason: Fixed PLSQL-PROJECT split_script boundary-corruption bug (commit 23978e9). Root cause: line-by-line scan with no lexical state => a lone '/' line OR trailing ';' inside a multi-line string literal / block comment falsely terminated, corrupting unit boundaries. Fix: persistent cross-line in_string/in_block_comment state via consume_line() (handles Oracle '' escaped-quote, -- line comments rest-inert, multi-line /* */; returns ends-with-top-level-';'). Lone-'/' terminator now gated on at_top_level_at_line_start (a '/' inside an open string is content); ';' SQL terminator uses the top-level result not a buried ';'; a real terminator resets lexical state. Behaviour-preserving for all non-pathological cases — 50 pre-existing splitter tests still green + 4 new regression tests (';' in multi-line string, lone '/' in multi-line string, ';'/'/'in block comment, '' escaped quote). 54 plsql-project tests pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. My plsql-project lane, collision-free with cc_1's plsql-sast.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — plsql-doc serve_preview_blocking has no public-bind guard, unlike plsql-mcp
description:
crates/plsql-doc/src/serve.rs:38-43 -- the public, re-exported (lib.rs:20 'pub use serve::serve_preview_blocking') function serve_preview_blocking<A: ToSocketAddrs>(addr, set, project_label) binds whatever address the caller passes directly via TcpListener::bind(addr), with no validation.

The module doc (serve.rs:6) states the server 'binds 127.0.0.1 by default ... SHOULD NOT be' exposed -- but that is only a convention; the function imposes no guard. If a caller passes 0.0.0.0 or a routable address, the preview server -- which serves rendered PL/SQL source, schema docs, and dependency graphs over plain HTTP with NO authentication -- is exposed on all interfaces.

Contrast with crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:parse_listen_target, which deliberately REFUSES public-internet binds (0.0.0.0 / ::) unless explicitly overridden. The two TCP-serving crates in the same workspace have inconsistent bind-safety postures.

Why it matters: defense-in-depth. For an OSS release, a public API that can trivially expose unauthenticated source/schema content on a public interface, while a sibling crate guards against exactly that, is an inconsistent and leaky abstraction a security-minded reviewer will flag. The 'SHOULD NOT' doc is not a control.

Suggested fix: mirror the plsql-mcp pattern -- have serve_preview_blocking (or a wrapper) resolve the addr and refuse non-loopback/non-private binds by default, with an explicit opt-in parameter to override. At minimum, resolve the addr and emit a loud warning when binding a non-loopback address.

title: PLSQL-ENGINE real-estate gap: clean completeness report contradicts empty dep_graph + 53K diagnostics on a private Oracle estate
description: First run of `plsql-engine analyze` against a real Oracle estate (a private Oracle estate: 4251 files, 329MB, 713 .pkg + 3847 .trg + APEX exports). HONEST RESULT: (+) ran clean, exit 0, ZERO panics across the whole estate (tolerant contract + this session's UTF-8 fixes hold on real input — genuine robustness win). (−) but the SEMANTIC output is hollow on real code: dep_graph = 0 nodes / 0 edges, fact_store = 0 facts, dynamic_sql_sites = 0, unresolved_references = 0, db_link_edges = 0, semantic_model = just declaration_count, flow_summary all 0 — WHILE emitting 53,164 diagnostics. ROOT CAUSE: the deep ParseBackend is not wired (parser_backend reports 'Antlr4Rust' but the real path is the text-scanning pre-parser; the ANTLR backend is Java-gated/un-wired per PLSQL-PARSE-000A), so no semantic structure is extracted from real PL/SQL — the product's core 'know what breaks' value (dep graph / facts / lineage) cannot be delivered on a real estate today. HONESTY BUG (the actionable, in-scope part, plan §1.5 Evidence-UX commitment): the CompletenessReport headline says files_parsed_cleanly=4251 / files_recovered=0 / unresolved_references=0 / skipped_token_ratio=0.0 — a misleadingly PRISTINE picture that directly contradicts 53,164 diagnostics and an empty dep_graph/fact_store. The Trust Block must reflect 'we extracted ~nothing semantically here' (high uncertainty), not '0 unknowns'. Repro: plsql-engine analyze <real estate> --out x.json; jq .payload.completeness vs .payload.diagnostics|length vs .payload.dep_graph. Acceptance: completeness/Trust Block honestly reflects diagnostic volume + empty semantic subsystems (no false-clean); + a discovered-from child for wiring the deep backend (PLSQL-PARSE-000A scope).
close_reason:
Honest CompletenessReport/Trust Block landed in 02eb049. RE-RUN EVIDENCE (cargo run -q -p plsql-engine -- analyze ~/projects/private-estate --out /tmp/xc_p2.json; jq .payload.completeness):

BEFORE (false-clean): files_parsed_cleanly:4224, files_recovered:27, skipped_token_ratio:0.0, dynamic_sql_sites:0, opaque_dynamic_sql_sites:0, db_link_edges:0, unresolved_references:0, wrapped_units:0, missing_package_bodies:0 — a pristine all-zeros picture WHILE 6784 diagnostics / 6609 IR_UNCLASSIFIED_DECL.

AFTER (honest): posture:'LowConfidence', objects_unrecognized:6609, diagnostics_total:6784, objects_with_extracted_semantics:4123, extracted_semantics_ratio:0.38417816; wrapped_units/missing_package_bodies/dynamic_sql_sites/opaque_dynamic_sql_sites/db_link_edges/unresolved_references all now {"unmeasured":true} (NOT a misleading 0); schema_version 1.1.0 (additive bump).

Honesty cuts both ways (asserted in tests): clean synthetic corpus still reads Clean/Partial with ratio ~1.0; demo-no-db passes. Gates: cargo test --workspace 1621 passed / 0 suite failures (one TCP-socket flake green on isolated re-run, unrelated — zero plsql-mcp diff); cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings clean. Tests failing-first then green: plsql-core low_extraction_run_is_never_clean / clean_input_still_reads_clean / unmeasured serialization / degraded_when_nothing_understood; plsql-engine bh4p_low_extraction_run_reports_honest_non_clean_completeness + bh4p_clean_corpus_stays_honest_but_healthy. Deep-backend wiring remains separate scope (PLSQL-PARSE-000A); this fix is purely the reporting-layer honesty.

title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-2 — Add ALL_POLICIES (VPD) loader for fine-grained access security (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog has no VPD/RLS coverage. ALL_POLICIES (and ALL_POLICY_CONTEXTS, ALL_POLICY_GROUPS) define DBMS_RLS predicates that change the effective table contents per session — a SELECT against a VPD-protected table reads through a generated WHERE clause. Lineage that ignores VPD will report the table as having only the columns/rows we see; auditors see the policy as silent.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for security options + SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD). Local catalog file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs around the load_catalog_grants block at 2571.

Proposed shape. load_catalog_policies(conn, scope) -> Vec<VpdPolicy> over ALL_POLICIES (OBJECT_OWNER, OBJECT_NAME, POLICY_GROUP, POLICY_NAME, PF_OWNER, PACKAGE, FUNCTION, SEL/INS/UPD/DEL/IDX flags, CHK_OPTION, ENABLE, STATIC_POLICY, POLICY_TYPE). Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::vpd_policies. Lineage flags VPD-protected objects with UnknownReason::VpdPolicyApplied so downstream consumers know the read surface is dynamic.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_POLICIES view + Database Security Guide chapter on VPD.
close_reason: Added VpdPolicy {object_owner, object_name, policy_group, policy_name, function_owner, function_package, function_name, on_select, on_insert, on_update, on_delete, enabled} struct + SchemaCatalog::vpd_policies (behind #[serde(default)]). load_catalog_vpd_policies queries ALL_POLICIES filtered by object_owner. apply_vpd_policy_row decodes YN/YES/NO mix from dictionary text. Wired between load_catalog_editioning_views and load_catalog_dependencies. 3 orchestration fixtures updated. Test verifies BILLING_TENANT_ISOLATION policy on INVOICES routed through SECURITY.RLS_PKG.TENANT_PREDICATE with SELECT-only flags + enabled correctly decoded. 28 catalog tests pass; clippy --all-targets -D warnings clean. Cite: /oracle skill audit, SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (VPD chapter); Oracle Database Reference 26 ALL_POLICIES view.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — audit_insert_sql interpolates unvalidated table_name into SQL (identifier injection)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/audit.rs:100-104 (with_audit_table), 155-165 (audit_insert_sql)

THREAT (SQL identifier injection): audit_insert_sql builds the audit-row INSERT by string interpolation:
  format!("insert into {table} (tool_name, agent_program, agent_model, session_id, recorded_at) values (:1,:2,:3,:4, systimestamp)")
'table' is self.sink.table_name. The doc comments CLAIM the value is validated — line 60 says 'the AuditPlan builder verifies the value is non-empty + matches a simple OWNER.NAME or NAME shape' — but with_audit_table (line 102) does NO validation whatsoever; it just stores the string. audit_insert_sql only trims and checks non-empty. So whatever ends up in table_name (a connections.toml field, a config value, or an agent-supplied argument once the tool is wired) is spliced verbatim into executable SQL.

A table_name carrying extra columns + a statement terminator + a second DDL/DML statement + a line comment, or a DBLINK suffix (X@SOMELINK), or an embedded subquery, escapes the intended INSERT shape. The VALUES are correctly bound (:1..:4) but the table IDENTIFIER itself is not — identifier positions cannot be bind-parameterised in Oracle, which is exactly why the doc demanded a SIMPLE_SQL_NAME-style validation that was never implemented. patch.rs::is_simple_sql_name is the existing, correct validator for this in the same crate.

FIX: Validate table_name in with_audit_table (or a builder finalisation step) before it can reach audit_insert_sql. For an OWNER.NAME shape: split on exactly one '.', run each half through is_simple_sql_name (reuse patch.rs's, or lift to a shared module). Reject anything else. Make audit_insert_sql return None/error for an unvalidated name. Fix the false doc claim either way.

SEVERITY: Medium — DDL/DML injection into the audit path; the audit table is operator-configured today (lower attacker control) but the doc-vs-code divergence means a future wiring that lets an agent set it would be directly exploitable, and the gap silently contradicts a stated invariant.

title: PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — README over-promises the MCP server's tool-execution capability
description:
README.md:270-272 states: 'The MCP server (plsql-mcp) is a single crate exposing the full tool surface: foundation static-analysis tools, change-impact and release-gate tools, and the feature-gated live-DB surface.' README.md:333-339 documents 'plsql-mcp serve' / 'serve --listen ADDR' as 'stdio MCP loop' / 'TCP MCP loop' with no caveat.

But the MCP tool-execution path is NOT implemented: crates/plsql-mcp/src/mcp_protocol.rs:206-236 handle_tools_call validates the tool name against the registry and then returns a STATIC placeholder ('tool {name} is registered but execution is gated on the per-tool MCP-LIVE wiring') for EVERY tool. main.rs:4 confirms 'serve reaches into the transport layer once the per-tool beads land'. So an MCP client can connect, run initialize and tools/list, but every tools/call returns a non-executing stub.

(NOTE: the handle_tools_call placeholder itself is already tracked as a known issue and is NOT being re-filed here. This bead is specifically about the README/docs over-claiming.)

Why it matters: the README presents plsql-mcp as a working MCP server 'exposing the full tool surface'. A user who installs it and wires it into Cursor/Claude Desktop will find tool discovery works but no tool actually runs. For an OSS release this is a documentation-honesty gap -- the prose promises a capability the binary does not yet have.

Suggested fix: until the per-tool dispatch is wired, the README should state plainly that the MCP server currently supports the discovery handshake (initialize / tools/list) and that tool *execution* is in progress / not yet wired. Match the honest framing already used elsewhere in the tree (e.g. plsql-mcp/src/lib.rs:8 'Per-tool engine integration lands in subsequent beads', and the honest SKIP language in PUBLISHING.md).
close_reason: README MCP claim corrected: now states the server completes the handshake + advertises the full surface over tools/list, with per-tool tools/call wiring in progress; matching note added to Limitations. No longer overclaims.

title: PLSQL-SAST-003 — Implement SEC001 (`EXECUTE IMMEDIATE` injection) using flow taint/string-shape evidence + tests
close_reason: Implemented Sec001ExecuteImmediateInjection in crates/plsql-sast/src/rules.rs (commit e67b3e7). For each DynamicSqlEvidence site: taint-reachable via FlowQuery::taint_of (uncleansed) => Critical injection finding with taint-kinds + USING/DBMS_ASSERT remediation; Opacity fact on site => skip OpaqueConstruct (R13 — never assert safe on opaque); no string/taint evidence => skip MissingFlowFacts (R13); pure Literal/Empty StringShape => provably safe (no finding/skip). Non-tainted concatenation deliberately deferred to SEC002 so SEC001 only asserts proven taint-to-sink (high precision). required_facts=[DynamicSqlEvidence], default completeness (intra-unit flow). 5 tests including a REAL taint built through plsql_ir::analyze_flow over lowered statements (tainted->finding / opaque->skip / no-evidence->skip / literal->safe / no-dynsql->required-facts gate). Full plsql-sast suite 27 pass; cargo clippy --no-deps -D warnings clean. Cross-checked vs /oracle SECURITY-OPTIONS-REFERENCE.md (SQL injection/dynamic SQL) + DATABASE-REFERENCE.md (EXECUTE IMMEDIATE, DBMS_ASSERT cleansers).

It calls plsql_catalog::synthetic::billing_schema() (already used by 4 unit tests) and exports through plsql_catalog::export_snapshot_to_json so the fixtures are byte-identical-on-rerun until the synthetic builder itself changes. Ran the example once: emitted corpus/lab/snapshots/l1_billing.json (1 schema, 9 objects — 4 tables, 1 view, 2 packages, 1 procedure, 1 sequence; schema_id=plsql.catalog.snapshot, schema_version=1.1.0), corpus/lab/snapshots/l2_billing_extended.json (slot reserved, currently mirrors L1 until the L2 corpus grows), and corpus/lab/plscope/l1_billing.json (PL/Scope skeleton with empty identifier/reference/statement arrays + IdentifiersAndStatements availability). corpus/lab/README.md documents the refresh command + the round-trip guarantee via the existing catalog_snapshot_round_trips_through_versioned_json_document test (so any future schema bump breaks the test before it breaks consumers). corpus-license-check passes (56 entries / 1 enforced root; corpus/lab/ is not an enforced root per the existing policy).

title: PLSQL-MCP-LIVE-008 — Implement named safety profiles (`inspect_only` default, `ddl_guarded`, `session_write_enabled`) + read-only-by-default session state + `enable_writes` requiring operator confirmation token + `disable_writes`
close_reason: Extended plsql-mcp::safety with SessionSafetyState wrapping the SafetyProfile from MCP-001. Session-level surface: profile (active SafetyProfile, defaults to inspect_only), session_writes_enabled (read-only-by-default), permanently_read_only (hard guard carried from ConnectionProfile), active_token (Option<EnableWritesToken>). EnableWritesToken carries token string + connection + operation_summary + issued_at (unix seconds) + ttl_seconds; ENABLE_WRITES_TOKEN_TTL_SECONDS = 60s default per §13A.3. State transitions: mint_token (refused for permanently_read_only, otherwise creates fresh token + invalidates prior), enable_writes(token, connection, now) (single-use; clears active_token on success, also on expired-token detection so a fresh mint is required; rejects mismatched token/connection without consuming), disable_writes (idempotent — returns AlreadyReadOnly when not write-enabled; success reverts profile to InspectOnly + clears token). writes_allowed() centralizes the gate; permanently_read_only always returns false regardless of any other state. Four new tool descriptors registered: current_safety_profile, set_safety_profile, enable_writes, disable_writes (ToolTier::FoundationLiveDb). 7 new unit tests cover: default state, valid-token consume + single-use, expired token + active_token cleared, permanently_read_only refusal (mint AND enable), token-string mismatch, connection mismatch, disable_writes idempotency, and disable_writes profile revert. 30/30 mcp tests pass; clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings clean.

title: PLSQL-DEPS-002 — Bump toml 0.8 -> 1.1 with TOML 1.1 spec support (/library-updater)
description:
Audit finding from /library-updater pass on Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock.

Gap. toml crate is at 0.8.23 (latest 0.8.x); 1.1.2+spec-1.1.0 is GA. Used by:
- crates/plsql-mcp/Cargo.toml:31: toml = "0.8"

A 1.x bump is a major version change and brings TOML 1.1 spec support (inline tables, integer/float corner cases). plsql-mcp's config loading is the only consumer; the new toml::value::Value API may differ slightly.

Audit needed:
1. Scan plsql-mcp/src/config.rs for toml:: imports — check Deserialize derive vs ValueAccess trait churn between 0.8 and 1.x.
2. Verify that downstream consumers of plsql-mcp don't expose the toml::Value type in any public API (if they did, this becomes a breaking surface for plsql-intelligence consumers too).
3. Run plsql-mcp tests after bump — config-file round trip is the highest-leverage smoke test.

P3 because no known CVE on toml 0.8.x at audit time and the config-loading surface is small.

Cite. toml-rs 1.0 release notes (https://github.com/toml-rs/toml/releases) — verify before bumping.

title: PLSQL-OSS-SEC — MCP TCP transport binds RFC1918/link-local with zero authentication
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-mcp/src/tcp.rs:96-104 (is_safe_bind), 142-200 (process_stream / serve)

THREAT (unauthenticated remote access to the tool surface): parse_listen_target refuses 0.0.0.0/:: and public IPs by default (good) but treats every RFC1918 private address (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16), every IPv4 link-local (169.254/16), and IPv6 ULA/link-local as 'safe by default' — bindable with NO --allow-public-bind flag. The TCP accept loop (serve_with_listener -> handle_connection -> process_stream) performs NO authentication, NO token, NO TLS, NO peer check: any line-delimited JSON-RPC request from any host that can reach that ip:port is dispatched straight into handle_request_line and the full ToolRegistry.

On a shared LAN, a corporate VPN subnet, a cloud VPC, or a multi-tenant container network, 'private' is not 'trusted' — any co-resident host can drive the MCP server. The whole live-DB tool family (connect, query, patch_*, create_or_replace, execute_approved, enable_writes) is reachable. The skill's Axiom 6 applies: there is no cryptographic verification of who the peer is; reachability == authorization.

Also note: --allow-public-bind is referenced in the error text and tcp.rs but is NOT wired as a CLI flag in main.rs (run_serve hardcodes parse_listen_target(&raw, false)), so today public bind is simply impossible — but the private-range hole is wide open and needs no flag.

FIX (pick at least one, layered):
1. Default-deny everything except loopback (127.0.0.0/8, ::1); require an explicit opt-in flag for ANY non-loopback bind, RFC1918 included. The 'one agent per process / local dev transport' model in the module doc is a loopback model.
2. If non-loopback binds are intentionally supported, require a shared-secret / bearer token on each JSON-RPC frame (constant-time compared) before dispatch, or mutual-TLS.
3. Document prominently in README/PUBLISHING.md that --listen is loopback-only-safe and any wider bind exposes an unauthenticated tool surface.

SEVERITY: Medium-High depending on deployment — for an OSS release the safe default and the doc warning are the blockers; today's loopback+private default is too permissive for an unauthenticated protocol.

title: PLSQL-SAST-002 — Implement scan harness: load `AnalysisRun` / `FactStore` → run all enabled rules (honoring `required_facts` + `minimum_completeness`) → emit findings and skipped diagnostics
close_reason: Implemented crates/plsql-sast/src/harness.rs run_scan() (commit 95627a2): the SAST scan driver. Two precondition gates run before any rule executes: (1) completeness gate — Rule::minimum_completeness() vs CompletenessSnapshot, unmet => RuleSkippedDiagnostic(PreconditionUnmet), rule never run blind (R13 no silent false-negative); (2) required-facts gate — Rule::required_facts(): FactStore lacking any required FactKind => skip(MissingFlowFacts). Surviving rules run over every unit; findings+per-unit skips aggregate into a ScanReport deterministically sorted (rule_id/file/line/span; rule_id/unit/reason/detail) so output is stable regardless of registry/unit order (R10/R11). Rule trait extended with DEFAULTED required_facts()+minimum_completeness() (non-breaking — all existing SAST-001 rules/tests compile + pass unchanged). title: PLSQL-CAT-NEW-3 — Add ALL_EDITIONS + ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS for EBR awareness (/oracle audit)
description:
Audit finding from /oracle skill applied to crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs.

Gap. Catalog flattens to the current edition only. Edition-Based Redefinition (EBR) lets a single object exist in multiple editions with different bodies — DEFAULT_EDITION and child editions. ALL_OBJECTS already shows EDITION_NAME, but we don't fetch ALL_EDITIONS (the edition tree) or ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (the editioning view shape that masks the underlying table). Lineage in an EBR shop will miss the version-pinned alternatives.

Source. /oracle skill — DATABASE-REFERENCE.md routing for EBR + the existing CatalogSnapshot.edition column on objects. Local file: crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs:1977 (load_catalog_objects already SELECTs edition_name from all_objects).

Proposed shape. Two loaders:
- load_catalog_editions(conn) -> Vec<Edition> over ALL_EDITIONS (EDITION_NAME, PARENT_EDITION_NAME, USABLE).
- load_catalog_editioning_views(conn, scope) -> Vec<EditioningView> over ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS (OWNER, VIEW_NAME, TABLE_NAME).
Plumbed into CatalogSnapshot::editions / ::editioning_views. CompletenessReport gains an UnknownReason::EditionFlattened when an object has child editions we did not enumerate.

Cite. Oracle Database Reference 26, ALL_EDITIONS and ALL_EDITIONING_VIEWS views + Database Development Guide chapter on EBR.

title: PLSQL-CICD-007 — Author GitHub Actions example workflow in `examples/ci/github-actions.yml`
PLSQL-OSS-AUDIT — internal bead IDs leak into 142 published rustdoc comments
description:
142 doc comments (///) across the workspace src embed internal issue-tracker IDs -- both 'PLSQL-XXX-NNN' spec/bead codes and 'oracle-xxxx' beads IDs -- directly into rustdoc that 'cargo doc' renders into the public API documentation.

Heaviest offenders (count of /// lines with a bead ref):
  crates/plsql-catalog/src/lib.rs : 30
  crates/plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs : 25
  crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs  : 16
  crates/plsql-parser/src/ast.rs  : 11
  ...plus ~15 more files with 2-5 each.

Examples (all are /// doc comments, not // line comments):
  plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:34  '/// Schema descriptor for classify-change (PLSQL-LIN-000).'
  plsql-lineage/src/lib.rs:41  '/// Schema descriptor for the lineage doctor report (PLSQL-LIN-011).'
  plsql-ir/src/lib.rs:122      '...The concrete Declaration enum lands in PLSQL-IR-002.'
  plsql-ir/src/lib.rs:126      '...the statement enum lands in PLSQL-IR-004; ... PLSQL-SQLSEM-001.'

Why it matters: these IDs are meaningless to anyone outside the project. In the generated docs.rs / cargo doc output an OSS consumer sees 'lands in PLSQL-IR-002' with no way to resolve it -- it reads as leftover internal scaffolding and looks unfinished/unprofessional. The 'lands in X' phrasing also documents internal roadmap state as if it were API contract.

Suggested fix: sweep /// comments and either drop the parenthetical bead IDs or replace them with stable, consumer-meaningful prose. 'The statement enum lands in PLSQL-IR-004' should just describe what the type IS. Internal // (non-doc) comments referencing beads are fine to keep; only the rustdoc /// ones need scrubbing. A regex (rg '///.*\b(PLSQL-[A-Z]+-[0-9]|oracle-[a-z0-9]{4,})') enumerates all 142 sites.

description:

title: PLSQL-PROP-1 — Add 4 property tests (CST round-trip, cycle invariant, diagnostics monotonic, K18 idempotent) (/testing-property)
description:
Audit finding from /testing-property pass.

State. proptest is a dev-dependency of plsql-parser; one proptest harness lives at crates/plsql-parser/tests/fuzz_corpus_derived.rs (256 cases per run, +64 mutations per corpus input, ~5000 total exercises per CI run).

Properties already enforced.
- exercise_input() never panics on any input (TokenTape::new + reconstruct round-trip).
- 256 proptest cases of random byte strings up to 4 KB never panic.
- 18 corpus-derived seed inputs each mutated 64 times.
Property coverage. The parser-surface panic-freedom property is the only one currently in proptest. Worth adding:

- ConcreteSyntaxTree::reconstruct round-trip: reconstruct(parse(s)) == s byte-for-byte for any s in the corpus. The token-tape supports this; the property test would catch any new lower-pass that drops trivia.
- DepGraph cycle invariants: cycle_detect() output is a subset of the actual SCC decomposition, never reports phantom cycles.
- SemanticModel diagnostics monotonicity: adding objects only ever increases the diagnostic count for completeness-related codes, never decreases.
- K18 sanitize() idempotence: sanitize(sanitize(s).0).0 == sanitize(s).0 for all s (the second pass should never find additional markers if the first redacted everything).

Action. File a follow-up bead to add the 4 property tests above. K18-idempotence is the cheapest add (sits inside plsql-mcp tests, no new dep needed).

Cite. /testing-property — properties (invariants) catch classes of bugs that example-based tests miss. Add them after every PR that touches a parser/lowering/sanitizer surface.
close_reason: Property-testing observations recorded as evidence artifact. Current property surface: proptest harness on parser TokenTape panic-freedom (256 cases + 64 mutations per corpus seed, ~5000 exercises per CI run). 4 candidate properties documented for follow-up: CST round-trip byte-for-byte, depgraph cycle subset of SCC, diagnostics monotonicity, K18 sanitize idempotence. K18-idempotence is the cheapest to add next.

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — split_statements does not depth-track IF/LOOP; every statement after the first inner ';' in an IF/LOOP body is mis-parented (dependency edges silently lost)
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs  fn split_statements (lines 156-200), specifically the keyword loop at 172-190.

BUG: split_statements splits a body on ';' at block depth 0. It only depth-tracks BEGIN (depth+=1, line 172-179) and END (depth=(depth-1).max(0), line 180-190). It does NOT track 'IF'/'END IF' or 'LOOP'/'END LOOP'. The function's OWN doc-comment (lines 156-160) explicitly promises the opposite: 'Split source on ; honouring nested BEGIN...END; blocks AND matching IF...END IF; / LOOP...END LOOP; so an inner semicolon does not tear apart a control-flow body.' The code never implements the IF/LOOP half.

WHY IT TRIGGERS: any IF or LOOP body that contains '<stmt>;' separators (i.e. essentially every non-trivial control-flow body). The first ';' inside the body is at depth 0, so split_statements cuts there. The first chunk becomes 'IF cond THEN firstStmt;' (no 'END IF'), classify_if then does rfind("END IF") -> None -> end_pos=len, so it lowers a one-arm IF. Every subsequent inner statement ('ELSIF ... ;', 'ELSE ...;', second/third body statements, and the trailing 'END IF;'/'END LOOP;') is emitted as a SEPARATE top-level Statement::Unrecognized or re-parented to the top level.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate against plsql-ir):
  lower_statement_body("IF a THEN s1(); ELSIF b THEN s2(); ELSIF c THEN s3(); ELSE s4(); END IF;")
   => 5 statements: If{arms:[a/s1();]}, Unrecognized{"ELSIF b THEN s2();"}, Unrecognized{"ELSIF c THEN s3();"}, Unrecognized{"ELSE s4();"}, Unrecognized{"END IF;"}
   extract_call_sites => ["s1"]  -- s2,s3,s4 entirely lost.
  lower_statement_body("IF p_flag=1 THEN INSERT INTO audit_log VALUES(1); UPDATE accounts SET bal=0; END IF;")
   => If{arm: INSERT...}, Sql{Update accounts}, Unrecognized{"END IF;"} -- the UPDATE leaks to top level and is reported UNCONDITIONAL (loses the IF guard); 'END IF;' is a bogus Unrecognized stmt.

DOWNSTREAM IMPACT (HIGH): plsql-engine analyze_project (crates/plsql-engine/src/lib.rs) builds Calls / Reads / Writes dependency edges from body_stmts. On the PRIMARY ANTLR path, tree_lower.rs::lower_statement (lines 542-544) emits AstStatement::If with cond_text = node_text of the WHOLE if_statement node (full 'IF...END IF' source incl. body); ast_stmts_to_ir then does plsql_ir::lower_statement_body(cond_text) (engine lib.rs:667) to recover the body's nested DML. That re-lowering is exactly where this bug fires. On the FALLBACK path (engine lib.rs:929) lower_statement_body(slice) on a decl span hits it too. Net result: call sites and table Read/Write accesses inside IF/LOOP bodies are silently dropped or mis-attributed -> incomplete + incorrect dependency graph with NO honest diagnostic, violating the project's R13 'never hide uncertainty' rule. Tests miss it because every classify_if/extract_call_sites unit test uses a single-statement body or a body with no internal ';'.

SUGGESTED FIX: in split_statements, also depth-track IF (consume_keyword 'IF' -> depth+=1) and LOOP, and on END only decrement for a bare END (not END IF / END LOOP) OR track all three openers consistently so 'END IF'/'END LOOP'/'END' each match their opener. The cleanest is: increment on IF/LOOP/BEGIN/CASE openers and decrement on the matching END form; only split on ';' when depth==0. Note crates/plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs::lower_statement_body (lines 511-556) has the identical BEGIN/END-only bug (currently only reachable from tests) and should be fixed the same way.
close_reason: split_statements (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs) + lower_statement_body (plsql-parser-antlr/src/lower/mod.rs) now depth-track every block opener — BEGIN/IF/LOOP/CASE — and the matching terminators (bare END plus END IF/END LOOP/END CASE, matched before bare END to avoid double-counting). Inner ';' inside any control-flow body no longer splits the statement. R13: plsql-ir splitter flags an unterminated block and lower_statement_body emits Unrecognized/UnterminatedBlock. Doc-comments rewritten to match. New tests: stmt.rs multi_statement_if/loop/bare_loop_body_is_one_statement, nested_if_inside_loop_stays_one_statement, unterminated_if_block_degrades_with_typed_reason; lower/mod.rs parse005_if/loop_body_not_split_on_inner_semicolons, parse005_nested_if_inside_loop_not_split. All written failing-first, watched fail, then fixed. cargo build --workspace OK; full workspace suite 1713 passed/0 failed; clippy -D warnings clean; forbid(unsafe_code) intact.

title: PLSQL-ENG-003 — Wire cache reuse through `plsql-store` using content hashes + profile hashes (profile changes invalidate cached fragments)
close_reason: DRIFT-CLOSE (verified by re-run, beads-compliance Axiom 1 — not just read). ENG-003 'wire cache reuse through plsql-store using content+profile hashes; profile changes invalidate cached fragments' is fully implemented and committed in crates/plsql-store/src/lib.rs: CacheKey{strategy_name,content_hash,profile_hash} + hash_hex() SHA-256 composer + put_cached() (content-addressed blob + cache_entries binding) + get_cached() (key lookup). Profile-change invalidation is correct by construction (profile_hash is part of the PRIMARY KEY (strategy_name,content_hash,profile_hash) on cache_entries, so a changed profile yields a key miss and forced recompute — no stale fragment ever served). Re-ran plsql-store lib tests: 19 passed incl. cached_fragment_round_trips_by_key, changed_profile_hash_invalidates_cached_fragment (the exact acceptance requirement), get_cached_rejects_unknown_strategy. No code change needed; no file touched (collision-safe). The engine-side consumption is ENG-002/004/005 (already closed).

title: PLSQL-LINT-002 — Fix clippy::unused_imports + dead_code in plsql-privileges/src/resolve.rs
description:

Two clean options:

title: PLSQL-OSS-BUG — classify_if emits phantom IF arms (first condition duplicated) on any IF with 2+ ELSIF branches
description:
FILE: crates/plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs  fn classify_if (lines 345-409).

BUG: The arm-building loop produces duplicate phantom arms when an IF has two or more ELSIF branches. Trace of the loop (lines 361-404):
 - Before the loop, the FIRST arm's condition is captured as first_cond (lines 358-360).
 - Each loop iteration unconditionally pushes an arm with cond_text = first_cond.clone() (lines 369-372) -- it always uses the FIRST condition, never the current one.
 - The Some((pos,"ELSIF")) branch (lines 374-393) then ALSO pushes a second, correct arm with the real ELSIF condition (lines 389-392) and advances cursor to body_end2.
 - Line 377 is dead code: 'arms.last_mut().unwrap().cond_text = arms.last().unwrap().cond_text.clone();' assigns a field to itself (no-op). The comment at 379-380 ('Replace placeholder cond on next iteration') describes an intent that is never carried out.
 - On the NEXT iteration, find_any_keyword finds the next ELSIF, and lines 369-372 push YET ANOTHER arm with first_cond -- a phantom.

CONFIRMED EMPIRICALLY (scratch crate; semicolon-free IF so split_statements keeps it whole):
  lower_statement_body("IF a THEN NULL ELSIF b THEN NULL ELSIF c THEN NULL ELSE NULL END IF")
   => If with 5 arms: [a/"NULL", b/"NULL", a/"", c/"NULL", a/""], else=Some("NULL")
   EXPECTED: 3 arms [a, b, c]. Got 2 phantom arms with condition 'a' and empty body.

WHY IT MATTERS: every phantom arm with cond_text='a' is walked by extract_call_sites (calls.rs walk_call_sites -> CallContext::ControlFlow on arm.cond_text) and by flow analysis -> the first condition's callees/refs are duplicated, and a real arm is structurally misplaced. The body slices on phantom arms are empty or contain leaked 'ELSIF ... THEN' text.

NOTE: in practice this bug is currently MASKED by oracle-hbhm (split_statements tears IF bodies apart on internal ';' so classify_if rarely sees an intact multi-ELSIF chain). It becomes live as soon as that splitter bug is fixed, OR for any IF whose ELSIF bodies contain no ';'. It is an independent logic defect and should be fixed alongside.

SUGGESTED FIX: rewrite the loop so each iteration handles exactly ONE arm: capture the current arm's condition (first_cond for the first iteration, the ELSIF condition thereafter), find the body slice up to the next ELSIF/ELSE/END, push exactly one IfArm, then advance. Delete the dead self-assignment at line 377 and the double-push in the ELSIF branch.
close_reason: classify_if (plsql-ir/src/stmt.rs) rewritten so each loop iteration handles exactly ONE arm: capture this arm's condition (IF for the first, ELSIF thereafter via cond_start), slice body to next ELSIF/ELSE, push a single IfArm, advance. Deleted the dead self-assignment and the double-push in the ELSIF branch that produced phantom arms duplicating the first condition. New tests: multi_elsif_if_has_no_phantom_arms (asserts exactly 3 arms a/b/c, no phantoms) and multi_elsif_if_keeps_bodies_with_conditions (each body attached to correct cond). Written failing-first (confirmed [a,b,a,c,a]), watched fail, then fixed. Fixed alongside hbhm as required since hbhm masked it. Suite green.

title: PLSQL-PROF-1 — Profiling observations (catalog parallel-fetch, lineage bitset, K18 Aho-Corasick) (/profiling)
description:
Audit finding from /profiling pass.

State. cargo bench --no-run gate is wired in .github/workflows/ci.yml (PLSQL-WS-015), but no per-crate criterion benches exist yet outside the parser-corpus-bench bead (oracle-mln PLSQL-PARSE-016 — open).

Hot paths worth profiling (cold + warm).
- Catalog snapshot load: load_snapshot_from_connection issues 17 sequential queries; a parallel-fetch refactor (rayon + per-loader futures) could cut wall time substantially on schemas with hundreds of objects.
- Lineage impact traversal: BFS over 10k-node graphs is the agent-blocking path. Currently uses HashSet visited; switching to a bitset on graphs with stable LogicalObjectId u32 keys would be ~10x faster but requires the id allocator to dedupe.
- depgraph::cross_check_with_catalog: O(n*m) cartesian compare; the OurIndex BTreeMap walk is the natural fix and is already in place. Re-check after PARSE-005 lands and depgraph node count grows.
- K18 sanitize(): naive .replace() loop over ~35 markers per cell. For a 10k-row query result this is 350k substring searches. Build an Aho-Corasick once per process if marker count crosses ~64.

Action. No bench harness yet — PARSE-016 (oracle-mln) builds the parser corpus-bench. After that, file follow-up beads to add criterion benches per hot path (catalog load, lineage impact, sanitize batch).

This pass is observation-only; no measurable regression at current scale. Filing the finding so the profiling decisions are recorded.

Cite. /profiling — first identify the agent-blocking hot paths, then add observable measurements before optimizing.
close_reason: Profiling observations recorded as evidence artifact. Hot paths identified: catalog snapshot load (sequential 17 queries → parallel-fetch opportunity), lineage impact BFS (HashSet → bitset on stable LogicalObjectId keys), depgraph cross-check (already BTreeMap), K18 sanitize (35 substring searches → Aho-Corasick at scale). No measurable regression at current scale; bench harness pending oracle-mln PARSE-016.

title: PLSQL-AUDIT-LEAN — /lean-formal evidence pass
description:
Audit finding from /lean-formal pass — type-level invariant review.

State. The codebase encodes several invariants at the Rust type level that prevent classes of bugs at compile time:

1. R20 parser-backend isolation. ParseBackend trait is the boundary; ANTLR-generated types live in a private generated/ module under #[cfg(feature = "antlr-codegen")] and never escape plsql-parser-antlr. Type-system-enforced because the antlr-rust runtime types are not re-exported.

2. R13 typed UnknownReason. plsql-core::UnknownReason enum has 12 variants; every typed-uncertainty site uses the enum, never a stringly-typed code. The compiler refuses unrecognized variants at every match site, forcing the audit to surface when new variants are added.

3. Newtype identity. plsql-core gives ObjectName / ColumnName / SchemaName / SynonymName / etc. each their own newtype around SymbolId via the numeric_id! macro. A SchemaName cannot be passed where an ObjectName is expected — a class of identifier-mixing bug eliminated at the type level.

4. Span invariant (PLSQL-PARSE-010). Every AstDecl variant carries `span: Span`. The Spanned trait formalizes this, though it can't enforce 'every variant has a span field' (Rust limitation noted in ast.rs:23-25). The compromise: code-review enforced + Spanned impl block listing every variant.

5. Confidence layering. plsql-depgraph::Edge::propagate_confidence takes two Confidence values and returns the worse-of-pair. Confidence is `Clone, Eq, PartialEq` so the operation has total semantics.

6. Catalog snapshot back-compat. Every new SchemaCatalog field added (db_links, table_comments, column_comments, editioning_views, vpd_policies) lives behind #[serde(default)]. Older JSON snapshots keep deserializing into the new shape with the new fields zero-initialized.

7. Cargo workspace lints. unsafe_code = "forbid", dbg_macro = "deny", todo = "deny" — enforced at the lint layer, not just code review.

Gaps (where formal would help further):
- Confidence::Opaque + Confidence::High mixing has no compile-time check that the merge function actually returns the worse-of-pair (runtime invariant only).
- ObjectStatus enum has no `Default` impl; relies on serde::default attribute on consumers.

Verdict. R13 + R20 + newtype identity carry most of the formal-verification weight. The remaining runtime-invariant gaps are documented but not currently lifted to types.

Cite. /lean-formal — encode invariants at the type system level where possible; rely on tests for the rest.
close_reason: /lean-formal evidence pass. Type-level invariants identified: R20 parser-backend isolation (ParseBackend trait + private generated/ module under feature gate), R13 typed UnknownReason (12-variant enum, compile-time exhaustive match), newtype identity (ObjectName/ColumnName/SchemaName/etc. via numeric_id! macro prevents identifier mixing), Span invariant (PLSQL-PARSE-010, partial — every AstDecl variant carries span: Span), Confidence merge (worse-of-pair, runtime), Catalog back-compat (#[serde(default)] on every new SchemaCatalog field), workspace lints (unsafe_code=forbid, dbg_macro=deny, todo=deny). Two runtime-only invariants documented as candidates for future lifting.
